🦒
Africa

Maasai Giraffe

Giraffa tippelskirchi
Status Endangered
Habitat Savannahs, Open Woodlands
Diet Herbivore
Lifespan 25-30 years
Weight 800-1,900 kg (male), 550-1,180 kg (female)
×

The Maasai giraffe (Giraffa tippelskirchi), also known as the Kilimanjaro giraffe, stands as the tallest terrestrial animal on Earth and the largest subspecies of giraffe. Native to East Africa, these magnificent creatures can reach heights of up to 5.5 meters (18 feet) for males, with their distinctive irregular, star-like patch patterns setting them apart from other giraffe subspecies. Their towering presence across the Kenyan and Tanzanian savannas represents one of nature's most remarkable evolutionary adaptations, allowing them to browse vegetation inaccessible to all other herbivores.

Habitat & Range

Maasai giraffes inhabit the savannas, open woodlands, and grasslands of East Africa, primarily in Kenya and Tanzania. They favor areas with abundant acacia trees, their primary food source, and require large home ranges of 50-500 square kilometers depending on resource availability. Key populations exist in the Masai Mara-Serengeti ecosystem, Amboseli National Park, Tsavo Conservation Area, and Ruaha National Park. Their distribution closely follows the availability of acacia woodlands and open browsing areas.

Behavior & Social Structure

Maasai giraffes are primarily solitary or found in loose, open herds with fluid membership. Unlike many social ungulates, they do not form tight family groups. Males establish dominance through 'necking'—swinging their necks at each other in ritualized combat. Females give birth standing up, resulting in a dramatic 2-meter drop for newborn calves. These calves can stand within 30 minutes and run within 10 hours. Giraffes communicate through infrasound below human hearing range and use body language extensively.

Diet & Nutrition

As specialized browsers, Maasai giraffes feed almost exclusively on acacia leaves, twigs, and bark, though they consume over 100 plant species. Their 50 cm prehensile tongue is specially adapted with thick, tough papillae to grasp and strip leaves from thorny branches. They can consume up to 34 kg of vegetation daily and obtain most of their moisture from food, allowing them to go days without drinking. Their height gives them access to food sources unavailable to other herbivores, reducing competition.

Key Facts

  • Tallest terrestrial animal, reaching up to 5.5 meters in height
  • Distinctive irregular, vine-leaf shaped patches unique to subspecies
  • Heart weighs up to 11 kg to pump blood to brain against gravity
  • Prehensile tongue extends up to 50 cm, specially adapted for thorny acacia
  • Requires only 5-30 minutes of sleep per day, often standing
  • Neck contains same number of vertebrae as humans (7), but each up to 25 cm long

Conservation Status

Classified as Endangered by the IUCN Red List. The Maasai giraffe population has declined by over 50% in recent decades, with fewer than 35,000 individuals remaining in the wild. Once abundant across East Africa, they now occupy less than 10% of their historic range. Primary threats include habitat loss and fragmentation from agricultural expansion, poaching for meat and hides, and human-wildlife conflict. Conservation efforts focus on anti-poaching patrols, habitat protection in key areas like the Masai Mara and Serengeti ecosystems, and community-based conservation initiatives that promote coexistence between giraffes and local pastoral communities.

Primary Threats

  • Habitat loss and fragmentation from agricultural expansion
  • Poaching for meat, hides, and tails (used as status symbols)
  • Human-wildlife conflict in populated areas
  • Drought and climate change affecting food sources
  • Lack of genetic diversity in isolated populations

Related Species

🐘 African Elephant 🦓 Grant's Zebra 🦁 Lion

Frequently Asked Questions

How big is an Maasai Giraffe?

Maasai Giraffes typically weigh 800-1,900 kg (male), 550-1,180 kg (female) and can be identified by their distinctive features. They are native to Savannahs, Open Woodlands.

Are Maasai Giraffes endangered?

The Maasai Giraffe is currently classified as Endangered by the IUCN Red List. Classified as Endangered by the IUCN Red List. The Maasai giraffe population has declined by over 50% in recent decades, with fewer than 35,000 indivi...

What do Maasai Giraffes eat?

Maasai Giraffes are herbivore. As specialized browsers, Maasai giraffes feed almost exclusively on acacia leaves, twigs, and bark, though they consume ...

How long do Maasai Giraffes live?

In the wild, Maasai Giraffes typically live for 25-30 years. Their lifespan can vary based on environmental conditions and conservation efforts.
